senses.definition: Medical term for conditions in which there is no hydrochloric acid in the stomach’s gastric juice.
examples: Prolonged use of certain medicines can lead to achlorhydrias in some patients.
Doctors may test for achlorhydrias when people have trouble digesting food.
Achlorhydrias are often linked to other serious stomach diseases.

senses.definition: Having no hydrochloric acid in the stomach’s gastric juice.
examples: An achlorhydric stomach cannot digest protein normally.
Achlorhydric patients may need vitamin B12 injections.
The study compared achlorhydric and non‑achlorhydric subjects.

senses.definition: Lacking chlorophyll, and therefore unable to carry out normal photosynthesis.
examples: The achlorophyllous seedlings depend on stored food reserves.
Some achlorophyllous plants live as parasites on the roots of other species.
Achlorophyllous leaves appear pale or white instead of green.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which a person cannot see colors normally, especially green.
examples: The patient’s achloropsia made it hard for him to distinguish traffic lights.
Achloropsia is usually diagnosed with special color vision tests.
Researchers are studying genetic causes of achloropsia.

senses.definition: An extreme and persistent fear of darkness.
examples: His achluophobia was so strong that he slept with several lights on.
Therapy can help people manage achluophobia.
Achluophobia often begins in childhood.

senses.definition: An ancient Persian city, usually identified with Ecbatana, mentioned in the Bible.
examples: The decree was said to be stored in Achmetha.
Scholars believe that Achmetha corresponds to the city of Ecbatana in Media.
In some translations, Achmetha is simply rendered as Ecbatana.

senses.definition: Nonstandard or dialect spelling of “a‑choke,” meaning to choke.
examples: He began to achoke on the smoke from the fire.
The actor pretended to achoke during the scene.
They worried the baby might achoke on small toys.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which little or no bile is made or enters the digestive tract.
examples: Acholia can cause pale or clay‑colored stools.
The doctor suspected acholia because the patient’s bile flow was reduced.
Acholia is often a sign of serious liver or bile duct disease.

senses.definition: Plural of acholia: conditions in which little or no bile is made or enters the digestive tract.
examples: Various liver disorders may present as different acholias.
The textbook describes several acholias associated with bile duct blockage.
These acholias often lead to changes in the color of the stool.

senses.definition: Lacking bile, especially describing pale clay‑colored stools.
examples: The infant was brought in with acholic stools and jaundice.
Acholic stools are a key warning sign of obstructed bile ducts.
Persistent acholic stools require urgent medical investigation.

senses.definition: A rare or obsolete term, probably relating to bile, with no stable modern meaning.
examples: The term acholoe appears only in older medical texts.
Modern doctors do not usually use the word acholoe.
The exact meaning of acholoe is unclear in current practice.

senses.definition: Containing no bile or very little bile.
examples: Acholous stools are often pale and difficult to recognize without experience.
The surgeon noted an acholous appearance of the intestinal contents.
Acholous digestive fluids may signal serious liver disease.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which there are no bile pigments in the urine.
examples: Acholuria can occur in some forms of jaundice.
The test results showed clear signs of acholuria.
Acholuria often appears together with pale stools and dark skin.
synonyms: absence of bile pigments in urine

senses.definition: Having no bile pigments in the urine, or relating to such a condition.
examples: The doctor diagnosed acholuric jaundice after reviewing the tests.
Acholuric patients may show pale urine but intense jaundice.
This form of anemia is sometimes described as acholuric.

senses.definition: The name of a Native American people from northern California, and their language.
examples: The Achomawi once lived along the Pit River in California.
Linguists have documented traditional stories in Achomawi.
Today, Achomawi descendants work to preserve their culture.

senses.definition: A type of stony meteorite that does not contain chondrules (small round mineral grains).
examples: Scientists classified the sample as an achondrite after detailed analysis.
Achondrite meteorites provide clues about early planetary crusts.
The museum’s rare achondrite attracted many visitors.

senses.definition: Plural of achondrite: stony meteorites that do not contain chondrules.
examples: Achondrites are less common than ordinary chondrites.
Many achondrites seem to come from differentiated parent bodies.
Researchers compared several achondrites from different collections.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the structure of an achondrite.
examples: The rock has an achondritic texture under the microscope.
Achondritic meteorites often show signs of melting and re‑crystallization.
This achondritic material may have formed on a small planet.

senses.definition: A genetic disorder that causes short stature with short arms and legs, while the trunk is of more normal size.
examples: Achondroplasia is the most common cause of dwarfism.
People with achondroplasia can live full lives with proper medical care.
Genetic counseling is often offered to families affected by achondroplasia.

senses.definition: Plural of achondroplasia: genetic disorders causing short stature with short limbs.
examples: Different achondroplasias may share similar physical features.
Researchers study various achondroplasias to understand bone growth.
These achondroplasias are all linked to changes in the same gene.

senses.definition: Having or relating to achondroplasia.
examples: He has achondroplastic dwarfism.
Achondroplastic features include short limbs and a larger head.
An achondroplastic child may need regular check‑ups with specialists.

senses.definition: A word used to represent the sound of a sneeze.
examples: "Achoo!" she sneezed loudly.
Every time he says "achoo," the dog runs away.
The baby let out a tiny "achoo" and everyone laughed.

senses.definition: A rare medical term for a scaly skin disease, especially affecting the scalp.
examples: Older medical books sometimes mention achor in children.
The doctor identified the crusts as signs of achor.
Achor is no longer a common diagnosis in modern dermatology.

senses.definition: Not having a notochord or spinal column.
examples: Some simple marine animals are achordal throughout their life cycle.
The larvae are achordal and show no trace of a backbone.
Achordal forms occupy a basal position in animal evolution.

senses.definition: A historical or obsolete name for a group of animals without a notochord.
examples: The term Achordata appears in older zoological classifications.
In that scheme, Achordata included all animals lacking a notochord.
Modern textbooks rarely use the name Achordata.

senses.definition: Lacking a notochord; not belonging to the chordates.
examples: Achordate animals include insects, worms, and many others.
The species is clearly achordate, with no supporting rod along the body.
Most marine invertebrates are achordate.

senses.definition: A genus name once used for fungi that cause certain scalp infections, now mostly replaced by other names.
examples: The fungus formerly known as Achorion was reclassified into a new genus.
Older reports describe Achorion schoenleinii as the cause of favus.
Today, the term Achorion is rarely used in clinical practice.

senses.definition: A former genus name for certain tropical fruit trees, now usually placed in the genus Manilkara.
examples: Achras zapota is now more often called Manilkara zapota.
The name Achras appears in older botanical literature.
Farmers planted achras trees for their sweet fruit.

senses.definition: A cry or shout used in Scottish Gaelic contexts, especially in historical battle cries, now chiefly literary.
examples: The warriors raised a fierce "achree" as they charged.
In the old song, the chorus ends with "Erin go bragh, achree."
The novelist used the word "achree" to give the dialogue a historic flavor.

senses.definition: A colourless cell, especially a white blood cell.
examples: The sample contained many achroacytes.
Achroacytes play an important role in the immune response.
Under the microscope, achroacytes appear nearly transparent.

senses.definition: A genus of small orchids, mainly from Australia and nearby regions.
examples: Achroanthes species often grow in open forests.
The botanist described a new Achroanthes from Tasmania.
Achroanthes orchids are valued by specialist collectors.

senses.definition: A form of dextrin that does not give a colored reaction with iodine.
examples: Heating starch can produce achrodextrin.
Achrodextrin remains colorless when tested with iodine solution.
The chemist identified the substance as achrodextrin.

senses.definition: An enzyme that breaks down achrodextrin.
examples: Achrodextrinase helps complete the digestion of dextrins.
Researchers measured the activity of achrodextrinase in the sample.
Lack of achrodextrinase may slow starch digestion.

senses.definition: The colorless protein part of a pigment such as haemoglobin, without its colored group.
examples: When the heme is removed, haemoglobin yields achroglobin.
The scientist isolated achroglobin for further study.
Achroglobin itself is nearly colorless in solution.

senses.definition: A condition in which red blood cells have too little color because they contain too little haemoglobin; anemia with pale cells.
examples: The blood test revealed achroiocythaemia in the patient.
Achroiocythaemia can cause tiredness and shortness of breath.
Iron supplements may help treat achroiocythaemia.

senses.definition: US spelling of achroiocythaemia: anemia in which red blood cells are paler than normal.
examples: Achroiocythemia was diagnosed after the blood film was examined.
Dietary changes can sometimes improve achroiocythemia.
The doctor explained that achroiocythemia means the cells lack normal color.

senses.definition: A colorless variety of the mineral tourmaline.
examples: The jeweler showed us a rare achroite crystal.
Achroite is valued by collectors but less used in jewelry.
The sample was identified as achroite under polarized light.

senses.definition: Loss or absence of normal color in a tissue or structure.
examples: The pathologist noted areas of achroma in the skin sample.
Achroma may indicate damage to pigment cells.
The disease can cause achroma of the hair and eyes.

senses.definition: A colorless cell, especially one without normal staining properties.
examples: The smear contained several achromacytes.
Achromacytes can be hard to see without special stains.
Researchers counted achromacytes separately from other cells.

senses.definition: Loss or absence of color; the quality of being without color.
examples: Achromasia in the tissue suggested a lack of pigment.
The disease leads to gradual achromasia of the hair.
Achromasia is sometimes evident in old photographs.

senses.definition: An achromatic lens designed to reduce color errors in an optical system.
examples: The microscope uses an achromat to give a sharp image.
We replaced the objective with a higher‑quality achromat.
Achromats are common in camera lenses and telescopes.

senses.definition: To make achromatic; to correct an optical system so that it shows little or no color error.
examples: Engineers tried to achromate the lens system for better image quality.
It is difficult to achromate very large lenses.
Designers achromate binoculars to reduce color fringes.

senses.definition: A family name in older classifications for certain colorless fungi or algae.
examples: Achromatiaceae appears in nineteenth‑century taxonomy books.
Modern systems no longer recognize Achromatiaceae as a distinct family.
The species was once placed in Achromatiaceae.

senses.definition: In optics, designed so that light of different colors comes to nearly the same focus, reducing color errors.
examples: The camera uses an achromatic lens to reduce color fringes.
Achromatic objectives improve the clarity of microscope images.
An achromatic system corrects for chromatic aberration.

senses.definition: Having no color or made only of neutral tones like black, white, and grey.
examples: The designer chose an achromatic color scheme for the room.
Achromatic clothing can look elegant and simple.
Photographers often work in achromatic tones to focus on shape and light.

senses.definition: In a way that reduces color errors in an optical system.
examples: The lenses are arranged achromatically to sharpen the image.
The system behaves achromatically over a wide range of wavelengths.
The telescope is designed to focus achromatically.

senses.definition: Using only neutral colors such as black, white, and grey.
examples: The artist worked achromatically to emphasize form.
The room is decorated achromatically with grey and white furniture.
He prefers to dress achromatically most days.

senses.definition: Plural of achromaticity: states or qualities of being free from color errors, or of being colorless.
examples: Different designs offer varying achromaticities across the spectrum.
The researcher compared the achromaticities of several lens systems.
These achromaticities make the instruments suitable for precision work.

senses.definition: The state of an optical system in which different colors are brought to nearly the same focus, reducing color errors.
examples: Achromaticity is essential in high‑quality microscope lenses.
Designers aim for achromaticity over the visible spectrum.
Improved achromaticity leads to sharper images.

senses.definition: The quality of having no color or being composed only of neutral tones.
examples: The achromaticity of the photograph draws attention to light and shadow.
Architects sometimes seek achromaticity in modern interiors.
Achromaticity can create a calm and minimal atmosphere.

senses.definition: The part of a cell nucleus that does not stain strongly and is not typical chromatin.
examples: The network of achromatin was visible between the dark chromatin granules.
Achromatin appears pale under ordinary nuclear stains.
Changes in achromatin can signal disease.

senses.definition: Not taking up ordinary nuclear stains; relating to achromatin.
examples: The achromatinic regions were difficult to see without special techniques.
Achromatinic fibers extend through the nucleus.
Certain diseases show increased achromatinic material.

senses.definition: Plural of achromatin: parts of cell nuclei that do not stain strongly.
examples: The slide showed several achromatins among the chromatin masses.
Researchers focused on the distribution of achromatins in the sample.
These achromatins form a fine network in the nucleus.
